does: This paper introduces Reverse Constitutional AI, a framework for systematically generating adversarial toxic data at scale without human annotation. The core method inverts a constitutional AI safety constitution into one that defines toxicity, then uses a critique-revision pipeline with reinforcement learning from AI feedback to iteratively refine model outputs toward toxic objectives. To prevent reward hacking and semantic degradation during optimization, the authors propose probability clamping, which constrains output probabilities to maintain coherence while pursuing adversarial goals. Experiments show the approach generates diverse, high-quality toxic data across multiple dimensions while improving semantic coherence by 15% through the probability clamping mechanism.
connects: This work extends constitutional AI methods, which have proven effective for aligning language models through principles-based feedback. Rather than using constitutional principles for safety, R-CAI inverts this logic to systematically explore failure modes, building on the red teaming tradition that seeks to identify vulnerabilities before deployment. The paper responds to limitations in existing adversarial data generation, which typically relies on isolated jailbreak prompts or expensive human annotation. It combines reinforcement learning from AI feedback with controlled optimization techniques, drawing on recent advances in using language models as critics and reward signals. The probability clamping innovation addresses known challenges with reward hacking in RL-based generation.
enables: This framework supports more comprehensive safety evaluation of language models by providing systematic, scalable access to diverse adversarial examples across multiple toxicity dimensions. It enables researchers to study failure modes and defenses without relying on limited human-generated jailbreaks or crowdsourced toxic data, potentially accelerating red teaming pipelines. The probability clamping technique generalizes beyond toxicity generation to any constrained optimization task requiring both goal achievement and output quality preservation, opening applications in controlled generation more broadly. Future work could extend this to other safety dimensions beyond toxicity, combine synthetically generated adversarial data with other training approaches, or investigate how models trained on such data respond to evolving attack strategies.
